data.title: Baird Q2 2025 International And Global Growth Fund Commentary
data.description:
MARKET RECAP

Global equity markets swung from steep losses to fresh highs during the quarter. In early April, President Trump imposed a baseline 10% tariff and reciprocal tariffs of up to 50% on dozens of trading partners, only to suspend most reciprocal tariffs for 90 days amid market panic. The U.S. and China de-escalated from extreme retaliatory measures in mid-May, which provided further relief to the markets.

Many countries have engaged with the U.S. on trade negotiations before the July 9 deadline; however, there is uncertainty whether deals could be struck by then and if a pause will be extended. Inflation has so far proved more benign than feared, but the widely held view is that tariff-induced price pressures will filter through supply chains in the coming months. Additionally, tariffs may yet threaten global economic growth.

This past quarter was a risk-on market environment, and we underperformed relative to benchmarks mainly due to our holdings in Asia & the Pacific Basin—especially in Greater China—and in some select defensives. We enter the new quarter cautiously optimistic but alert to continuing policy risks. Our investment strategies focus on the long term, allowing us to navigate short-term economic fluctuations. We prioritize businesses that align with secular trends and have strong competitive advantages and market positions. Our portfolio companies are chosen for their high profit margins, strong balance sheets, and consistent cash generation. We believe these qualities will endure even in challenging macroeconomic conditions. In our opinion, our investment process is not affected by tariffs, and the welldefined characteristics of our portfolio companies mean they should be better able to withstand external economic shocks.

Our concentrated, conviction-weighted portfolios are designed to outperform market growth rates over an investment cycle. Additionally, our portfolios are diversified across a wide range of secular growth themes. For instance, within the top ten holdings of our international strategy, in addition to holdings in artificial intelligence (AI), themes include industrial automation, financial services in emerging markets, e-commerce, mobile gaming, and digitalization.

In the second quarter of 2025, the Baird Chautauqua International Growth Fund Net Investor Class returned +7.72%, underperforming the MSCI (MSCI) ACWI ex-U.S. Index® ND, which returned +12.03%. The Baird Chautaugua Global Growth Fund Net Investor Class returned +9.07% during the quarter, underperforming the MSCI ACWI Index® ND, which returned +11.53%.*

MARKET UPDATE

For the MSCI ACWI ex-U.S. Index®, growth style outperformed value style. Within the MSCI ACWI Index®, growth style significantly outperformed value style, and large capitalization stocks underperformed small capitalization stocks. In emerging markets, growth style also outperformed value style.

Sector performance was mixed but leaned positive for the quarter, and country performance was mostly positive.

In the second quarter, global equity markets fell sharply before rebounding to new highs. April 2 was "Liberation Day," and President Trump unveiled a raft of tariffs that were significantly more severe than anyone had expected, including a 10% baseline universal tariff and additional reciprocal tariffs as high as 50% on dozens of trading partners. Reciprocal tariffs on China jumped to 145% in a matter of days, following swift tit-for-tat escalations. That announcement raised the prospect of higher consumer prices, a pullback in corporate and household spending, and heightening recession risks.

Just one week later, and following a violent market reaction, President Trump enjoined a 90-day pause on the reciprocal tariffs—except for China—to allow for trade negotiations to occur. The pause kicked off a market recovery from the Liberation Day lows. Following the initial shock and panic, markets became more confident that President Trump would back off from the worst-case tariff scenarios if necessary. Both the U.S. and international markets closed the guarter at or near all-time highs. However, concerns about the U.S. tax bill and its implications on the massive deficit helped weaken the dollar to multi-year lows.

Although tariffs did come into effect starting in February on China, Canada, and Mexico, as well as on autos, steel, and aluminum, the effect on U.S. inflation to date has been milder than feared, defying concerns that tariffs would rekindle inflation. Benign inflation readings through May suggest companies are finding ways—at least for now, including drawing down pre-tariff stockpiled inventories to avoid price hikes.

Meanwhile, consumer confidence has recovered a bit, though it is still lower than last year's, and consumer spending has weakened significantly. Business confidence has also fallen, but that sentiment never fully translated in the labor market. The U.S. added more jobs in May than expected, albeit at a slower pace, and the unemployment rate held steady. With inflation contained and the labor market intact, the Federal Reserve (Fed) kept interest rates steady, signaling that it views growth and price stability as balanced for now but that tariff-related aftereffects on inflation and the labor market are potentially looming.

The U.S. and China agreed to de-escalate trade tensions in mid-May. However, each side then accused the other of breaching the accord by using non-tariff measures to throttle critical exports—the U.S. accused China of intentional delays on export licenses for rare earths and magnets, and China flagged U.S. restrictions on jet engines and semiconductor design software. The two countries restored the accord in June, with both sides lowering tariffs and loosening key export restrictions, with the goal of working toward a broader trade deal. The U.S. lowered tariff rates on Chinese goods from 156% to 30%, and China lowered tariff rates on U.S. goods from 125% to 10%.

The trade war has made an immediate impact on China's export figures. After front-loading exports to the U.S. in March to avoid higher tariffs, exports to the U.S. plunged by 21% in April and 35% in May. However, overall export volumes have held up relatively well, as China pivoted to alternative markets such as Southeast Asia and Europe. This pivot may be evidence of re-routing, given that these regions benefited from reciprocal tariff pauses earlier and had been subject to only the universal baseline tariff. Other economic data in China painted a somewhat mixed picture. Manufacturing activity contracted for a third straight month in June, and consumer prices slipped into mild deflation, while retail sales grew 6% thanks to a government-subsidized trade-in program for consumer goods.

The People's Bank of China delivered some monetary support measures in response to the new tariffs and to stimulate the economy. These included a 10 basis point cut to its seven-day reporate, a 50 basis point reduction in banks' reserve requirement ratio, and some expanded lending facilities for services and consumer sectors. Yet more aggressive monetary or fiscal measures may be needed if China is to hit its roughly five percent growth target amid a protracted property slump and sluggish labor market.

Meanwhile, Europe countered trade war headwinds with ambitious fiscal stimulus and monetary easing. NATO members agreed to raise defense spending to 3.5% of GDP and allocate an additional 1.5% of GDP to security-related items. This has the potential to add hundreds of billions of euros in deficit-funded outlays per year over the next decade. Additionally, Germany had significantly loosened its fiscal restraints to support a €500 billion infrastructure investment program. On the monetary side, the European Central Bank cut interest rates to 2% and signaled that its easing cycle was nearing an end.

PERFORMANCE ATTRIBUTION

The Baird Chautaugua International Growth Fund underperformed its benchmark during the guarter as stock selection in information technology and industrials, as well as holdings and a relative overweight in health care, were the largest relative detractors. The portfolios' holdings in consumer discretionary and lack of exposure to energy contributed the most to relative returns. Regionally, holdings in Asia & the Pacific Basin—namely China—detracted the most, while stock selection in Europe—including the Netherlands, Ireland, and France-contributed most to relative returns. The largest detractors to relative returns in the portfolio were BeOne Medicines (ONC) (fka BeiGene), Waste Connections (WCN), and KE Holdings (BEKE). The largest contributors were Ryanair (RYAAY), Fairfax (OTCPK:FRFHF) Financial, and Safran.

The Baird Chautauqua Global Growth Fund underperformed its benchmark during the quarter as stock selection in information technology and a relative overweight in health care were the largest relative detractors. Lack of exposure to energy and consumer staples, along with holdings in utilities and financials, contributed the most to relative returns. Regionally, holdings in Asia & the Pacific Basin—namely China—detracted, while stock selection in Europe—including the Netherlands, Ireland, and France—contributed most to relative returns. The largest detractors to relative returns in the portfolio were NVIDIA, Waste Connections, and Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN). The largest relative contributors were Micron Technology (MU), Ryanair, and Safran.

Largest Contributors

Ryanair Holdings

After posting an in-line March quarter, Ryanair's outlook for the peak summer travel season was stronger than expected. Summer travel demand is robust, with peak fares trending +5-6% y/y. The medium-term pricing outlook is favorable as the industry is capacity-constrained to 2030. Ryanair is well-positioned to capitalize given its best-in-class unit cost position and balance sheet. The company's financial strength is underscored by the launch of a EUR 750 million share buyback program.

Safran

Safran reported first quarter revenue that was up 17% , exceeding expectations across all divisions. With Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) and Boeing (BA) continuing to face difficulties increasing production of new aircraft to meet demand, airlines are utilizing their existing fleets at higher rates for longer, underpinning solid engine aftermarket volumes and pricing over the short and medium term. Safran raised its 2025 engine spare part volume guidance and plans to implement a high-single-digit price increase in August.

Fairfax Financial Holdings Limited (International)

Fairfax posted another quarter of solid premium growth and underwriting performance despite significant catastrophe losses related to the Los Angeles wildfires. Fairfax's ability to absorb such a large catastrophe loss while still generating an underwriting profit is a testament to the insurance operations' scale and diversification, as well as the experience and skill of its management team. Fairfax grew book value per share 14% y/y and continued to return capital to shareholders via share repurchases.

Micron Technology (Global)

U.S. semiconductor companies performed well in 2Q25, with the Philadelphia Semiconductor Sector Index (SOX) up 29% in the quarter. Micron gained 42% thanks to strong sales growth in high-bandwidthmemory (HBM) DRAM, which is essential for AI computing.

Largest Detractors

Waste Connections, Inc.

Waste Connections reported a strong 1Q25 driven by solid waste pricing; however, shares fell 9% in 2Q after a 14% gain in 1Q. There may have been some investor disappointment that management did not raise guidance for the year. Recycled commodity prices declined 10%, but recycling represents only 2% of total revenues. Overall, the company is performing well operationally.

BeOne Medicines Ltd. (International)

After a 47% appreciation in 1Q25, BeOne Medicines fell 11% in 2Q, likely due to profit-taking and potential tariff concerns. BeOne Medicine, previously known as BeiGene, adopted its new name and redomiciled to Switzerland in late May. It achieved first-time GAAP and non-GAAP net profits in 1Q25, beat consensus estimates, and maintained its full year 2025 guidance.

KE Holdings, Inc. (International)

KE Holdings reported a solid 1Q25. The stock was impacted by tariff announcements and their potential secondary impact on the Chinese economy and real estate prices. The company's guidance for 2Q25 came in below consensus. Management signaled for continuous cost optimization in the future. We remain positive on the stock over the long term.

NVIDIA Corporation (Global)

Nvidia reported first quarter results that were extremely solid. The company took a write-down on China-specific datacenter products and flushed out any future China contributions from their guidance, following the new export restrictions introduced in April. Demand commentary ex-China was extremely encouraging—Nvidia is outgrowing expectations despite supply constraints and outgrowing competing ASIC products by a large margin. We have been underweight Nvidia relative to the benchmark, which was up 46% in the quarter, given our short-tomedium-term concerns that the feverish AI datacenter build may be resulting in overcapacity, which has not come to bear.

Regeneron Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (Global)

Regeneron reported mixed top-line results from a pipeline drug, itepekimab, in Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD), with one of the two trials meeting expectations. While itepekimab is not expected to be a core driver of Regeneron's shares, other drug candidates in its pipeline continue to advance.

PORTFOLIO HIGHLIGHTS | BUYS AND SELLS

For the Baird Chautauqua International Growth Fund, 88% of companies that reported earnings during the quarter were in-line with or exceeded consensus estimates.

For the Baird Chautauqua Global Growth Fund, 91% of companies that reported earnings during the quarter were in-line with or exceeded consensus estimates.

Our conviction weighting process, which considers our estimates for growth, profitability, and valuation, is key to our portfolio management strategy and has been additive to returns over the long run.

In the International Fund, we exited our position in Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing and reduced positions in BeOne Medicines, Constellation Software (OTCPK:CNSWF), Fairfax Financial, HDFC Bank (HDB), Sea Limited, and Waste Connections. Proceeds were used to initiate a new position in Lululemon (LULU) and increase positions in Atlassian (TEAM), Canadian Pacific, Novo Nordisk (NVO), and Ryanair.

In the Global Fund, we exited our positions in Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing and Illumina (ILMN) and reduced positions in BeOne Medicines, Fairfax Financial, HDFC Bank, and Sea Limited. Proceeds were used to initiate a new position in Lululemon and increase positions in Canadian Pacific, Novo Nordisk, Ryanair, and Universal Display (OLED).

OUTLOOK

With trade talks still in progress, the ultimate shape of global tariff policy is far from settled. The current 90-day suspension of reciprocal tariffs expires on July 9, though White House officials say it could be extended if agreements are close. Even so, any country that fails to reach a deal risks much higher duties. And while markets generally expect scaled-back versions of "Liberation Day" tariffs, it would still be enough to pose a downside risk to global economic growth. The coming months will test the patience of markets, the resolve of policymakers, and the resilience of consumers in the U.S. and abroad. In the absence of a clear resolution, tariff threats may persist as a driver of uncertainty, delaying decisive action by central banks and weighing on spending.

The U.K. has already inked a preliminary accord, making it something of an outlier. The E.U. has been working toward an agreement in principle with the U.S. that would permit discussions to continue past the July deadline. It has indicated its willingness to accept the 10% universal tariff on many bloc exports, provided the U.S. grants lower rates for strategic sectors such as pharmaceuticals, alcohol, semiconductors, and commercial aircraft. Additionally, the E.U. is pressing for quotas and exemptions that would effectively reduce sectoral tariffs on autos, steel, and aluminum. While the proposed framework favors the U.S., European officials believe it is a compromise they can live with. Canada has been able to resume talks after it rescinded a digital services tax on U.S. tech companies. Additionally, negotiations with Mexico, India, Japan, Vietnam, and other trading partners continue.

It is widely expected that tariffs will contribute to higher consumer prices in the coming months, a view that has restrained the Fed from cutting interest rates. Yet, tariff-induced price increases have been modest so far, casting doubt on how pronounced the inflationary effects will be. President Trump has publicly pressured Fed Chair Jerome Powell to lower rates. Within the Fed, opinions diverge sharply. There is great uncertainty over when and how much businesses may pass along the cost of higher import taxes into consumer prices. The concern is that higher tariffs might derail the Fed's yearslong fight to defeat inflation. Some Fed officials argue that any rate cuts should wait until tariff-induced cost pressures fully materialize, while others argue that rate cuts are warranted relative to current inflation rates. New economic projections released recently reflect this internal schism: among 19 rate-setters, 10 foresee at least two rate cuts this year, seven anticipate none, and two expect just one.

Powell acknowledged that while inflation pressures have been muted so far, significant price increases could emerge in June, July, and August as tariffs filter through supply chains. He also conceded that the actual impact might prove smaller than projected, a possibility that would weigh heavily on future policy decisions. A misstep—either cutting rates prematurely or maintaining restrictive policy in the face of contained inflation—could lead the Fed into more decisive moves later.

Meanwhile, the health of U.S. consumers looms as the most immediate risk to the U.S. economic outlook. Data released by the Commerce Department revised first quarter consumption growth down sharply to an annualized 0.5% from an initially reported 1.8%. The softness has persisted into the second quarter, with consumer spending declining consecutively in April and May. Discretionary categories have been hit especially hard, signaling that consumer confidence may be fraying under the weight of economic uncertainty.

China recognizes the urgency of bolstering domestic demand. The government announced a plan to stimulate consumption by boosting wages, increasing pensions, introducing financial incentives for childbirth, and shoring up stock and property markets. Yet, concrete policy details have been scant. Chinese households remain cautious amid a protracted downturn in the property sector and ongoing labor-market challenges. Bigger picture, years of talk about shifting toward a consumer-driven growth model have yielded little progress, and the trade war with the U.S. only magnifies the need for a more balanced economy.

President Xi faces a dilemma. On one hand, he aspires to make China self-sufficient in critical technologies-ranging from electric vehicles and batteries to construction machinery to health care—while also extending his country's influence in global supply chains. On the other hand, he has resisted broad handouts to consumers, wary of fostering "welfarism." As a result, the structural reforms required to strengthen the social safety net, raise household incomes, and pivot the financial system toward supporting consumer finances have stalled. Without such measures, China risks remaining overly reliant on industrial investment and exports, which can be a precarious position in the midst of protracted trade tensions.

In the International Fund, roughly 15% of assets are invested in Greater China* holdings, which is overweight relative to the benchmark. In the Global Fund, roughly 10% of assets are invested in Greater China* holdings, which is overweight relative to the benchmark. We believe our Chinese holdings are at valuation levels, in the context of their long-term growth outlooks and competitive positioning, that more than compensate us for the risks. Our Chinese holdings are exposed to secular growth areas of the domestic economy (private consumption and health care) that align with government priorities, have strong balance sheets and resilient cash flows, and are not reliant on restricted Western technology inputs for future growth.

Our investment strategy focuses on companies that benefit from long-term secular trends and have strong competitive advantages and market positions. Additionally, we have deliberately chosen companies with healthy profit margins, robust balance sheets, and consistent cash flow generation. Essentially, we have selected portfolio companies that we consider to be financially stable, even in challenging times. As a result, we believe our portfolios have the capacity to surpass market growth rates in the long run. In our opinion, our investment process is not affected by tariffs, and the well-defined characteristics of our portfolio companies mean they should be much better able to withstand external economic shocks.

Some of the most promising growth opportunities over long investment horizons may not be heavily influenced by current global events or specific regional circumstances. These opportunities include our investments in and around cloud computing, software-as-a-service, digital transformation, AI, semiconductor technology, e-commerce, payment systems, industrial automation, electric vehicles, and innovative biologic and biosimilar therapies. Additionally, there are other exciting growth prospects related to the rapid expansion of consumer markets, particularly in emerging economies and notably in Asia, which are driving the demand for various consumer products and financial services. Slowing global economic growth should not undermine the enduring strength of these investment themes, or the business models and market positions of the companies in our portfolios.

U.S. market valuations remain significantly elevated, with the cyclically adjusted price-to-earnings (CAPE) ratio recently reaching levels near historical peaks. In contrast, international markets trade at considerably lower valuations, offering a better starting point for expected future returns. Thus, we remain strategically positioned with a preference for international equities. We believe that our selective approach and emphasis on quality will effectively mitigate tariff-related risks while capitalizing on secular growth and valuationdriven opportunities.

BUSINESS UPDATE

There have been no changes to the investment team at Chautauqua Capital Management nor have there been changes to the ownership structure of our parent company, Baird.

data.title: Allspring Index Asset Allocation Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly review

  • The fund underperformed its benchmark, the Index Asset Allocation Blended Index, during the second quarter.
  • The fund's stock allocation performed in line with the S&P 500 Index, while the fixed income allocation outperformed the Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Index.
  • The fund's tactical asset allocation (TAA) trades detracted from performance over the period.

Market review

The second quarter of 2025 was marked by volatility in both asset returns and the macroeconomic narrative, reflecting the market's struggle to reconcile shifting economic data with evolving policy signals and geopolitical developments. Markets entered the quarter with a cautious tone, driven by concerns that both inflation and unemployment could worsen globally. "Liberation Day" on April 2 triggered a burst of highly volatile trading, and while the rollback of tariffs with China helped equities and credit spreads recover, underlying market dynamics remained unsettled. U.S. equities rebounded sharply from an early-April drawdown of over 10%, rallying 25% off the lows. Global equities generally kept pace but failed to sustain the relative performance seen earlier in the year.

Since the first quarter, a gradual decoupling in cross-asset correlations has emerged, particularly involving the U.S. dollar, which depreciated 7% over the quarter and 11% year to date. Sovereign bonds struggled amid conflicting narratives around fiscal restraint and monetary easing. The U.S. 10-year yield ended the quarter nearly unchanged but traded in a wide range between 4.00% and 4.60%, while global yield curves continued to steepen. Meanwhile, geopolitics has remained a key driver of commodity markets, with oil prices fluctuating within a wide $20 range. As the third quarter begins, markets remain highly reactive to policy shifts and macroeconomic surprises, reinforcing the need for nimble positioning.

data.description:
Quarterly review

  • The fund outperformed its custom benchmark, a blend of the MSCI All Country World Index (ACWI) ('net') (25%); the ICE BofA High Yield U.S. Corporates, Cash Pay Index (60%); and the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (15%), during the quarter that ended June 30, 2025.
  • Selection in our global equity allocation contributed to the outperformance.
  • An overweight to equity contributed to the outperformance for the quarter.

Market review

The second half of 2025 begins with markets still contending with a complex macroeconomic backdrop. U.S. growth has slowed, inflation expectations have risen due to tariff-related pressures, and the Federal Reserve (Fed) faces a difficult balancing act between supporting demand and containing price risks. While the market is pricing in multiple rate cuts, the Fed may proceed more cautiously, especially if inflation proves persistent. The July 4 signing of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act adds a degree of policy clarity—regardless of one's view of the legislation—and with its passage, investor attention is likely to shift more squarely toward the evolving tariff landscape and its economic implications. Globally, trade tensions and fiscal retrenchment are weighing on growth, and monetary easing is expected to continue across Europe and Asia.

In this environment, volatility is likely to persist, but so, too, are opportunities. Within fixed income, higher-quality spread sectors have continued to offer attractive income and relative stability. In equities, the divergence between U.S. large caps and the rest of the market remains notable. International and emerging market equities may benefit from a weaker U.S. dollar and shifting capital flows, while valuation gaps in U.S. small- and mid-cap stocks present potential entry points for selective investors. As uncertainty remains a defining feature of the landscape, we believe that a focus on fundamentals and flexibility in positioning will be essential.

data.title: S&P 500 Snapshot: The Jackson Hole Rally
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500 ended its five-day losing streak on Friday, posting its largest daily gain in nearly three months after the Jackson Hole rally. The index finished the week up 0.3%, its fifth weekly gain in the past six weeks. Here is a snapshot of the index from the past week:

The table below summarizes the number of record highs reached each year dating back to 2013.

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past six months with a 50-day moving average:

S&P 500: A Perspective on Drawdowns

On October 9, 2007, the S&P 500 reached a then all-time high, closing the day at 1565.15. Then on March 9, 2009, the index dropped ~57% off of its high from exactly 17 months before, closing the day at 676.53.

This time period became known as the Global Financial Crisis. It took over 5 years before the index reached a new then all-time high on March 28, 2013, where it closed out at 1569.19. The chart below is a snapshot of record highs and selloffs since the 2007 peak reached on October 9, 2007.

What happens if we take out the Global Financial Crisis? Here's a snapshot of the same chart above where the start date has been changed to the trough reached on March 9, 2009. Note the recent selloffs in 2022.

Here are a few tables with the number of days of a 1% or greater change in either direction and the number of days of corrections (down 10% or more from the record high).

And here is a linear chart of the index since October 9, 2007:

Here is a linearly scaled version of the same chart with the 50- and 200-day moving averages. The index has been above the 50-day moving average since May 1st and above the 200-day moving average since May 12th. Additionally, the 50-day moving average has been above the 200-day moving average since July 1st.

S&P 500: A Perspective on Volatility

For a sense of the correlation between the closing price and intraday volatility, the chart below overlays the S&P 500 since 2007 with the intraday price range.

On April 9th, the index experienced its largest intraday price volatility (10.77%) since December 24th, 2018 (19.10%). Also included is the 20-day moving average to identify trends in volatility. Over the past 20 days, the average percent change from the intraday low to the intraday high is 0.87%.

S&P 500 versus S&P Equal Weight

The S&P 500 is a market cap-weighted index that includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks spanning 11 sectors. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index includes the same constituents as the S&P 500, but each company is equally weighted at a fixed weight. So how do these two indexes match up against each other this year?

The S&P 500 is currently up 10.20% year to date, while the S&P Equal Weight is up 8.28% year to date.

ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG), and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP).

data.title: BJ's Restaurants Might Become More Efficient Than Analysts Expect
data.description:
I’ve always thought of BJ’s Restaurants Inc (NASDAQ:BJRI) as a casual-dining pizzeria, and that’s a common viewpoint in Southern California. But in other parts of the country, this restaurant is better known for its pizookies, large freshly baked cookies topped with ice cream. The cookies are so popular that the restaurant lost money on a promotional deal last year because diners with discount passes showed up more often than it expected.

BJ’s Restaurants introduced a value meal with a pizookie that helped it achieve 2.9 percent same-store sales growth in the second quarter. But that figure includes 3.3 percent traffic growth, which means average transaction size was down 0.4 percent. And BJ’s already has a much lower profit margin than other casual-dining restaurants. So the bull case for BJ’s depends on whether it can get more efficient. I believe that BJ’s strong recent earnings results, as well as its ongoing efficiency initiatives, show that it is getting more efficient.

How BJ’s Performs on Financial Metrics Versus Its Peers

BJ’s has an operating margin of 5.86%, which is fairly low for a casual-dining restaurant. For comparison, Darden Restaurant Inc.'s (DRI) margin is 13.17%, Brinker International's (EAT) margin is 8.67%, and The Cheesecake Factory Incorporated's (CAKE) margin is 6.91%. This low margin is preventing BJ’s from expanding right now, even though it can afford to, because it doesn’t want to add new restaurants if they won’t be profitable. For the time being, it’s been investing in remodeling its restaurants instead.

The other factor that stands out in BJ’s financial metrics is that this restaurant has much less debt than its peers. BJ’s has a debt-to-equity ratio of 124%. The numbers for Darden, Brinker, and The Cheesecake Factory are 338%, 457%, and 524% respectively. Now that interest rates have gone up, restaurants will have to roll over their loans at higher interest rates, and BJ’s is well positioned here.

BJ’s does have a higher forward P/E ratio than its peers at 20x. Even though both Brinker and The Cheesecake Factory have had huge rallies recently, their forward P/E ratios are currently 15x and 17x. Darden’s forward P/E is 19x. These metrics indicate that analysts currently believe BJ’s will continue to operate less efficiently than its peers, so if that changes this stock could rally. Meanwhile, analysts’ current one-year price target for this stock is $42, which is 24% higher than its current price of $34.

How BJ’s Is Getting More Efficient

As you might expect, analysts asked BJ’s management a lot of questions about how they’re making their restaurant more efficient on the second-quarter earnings call. BJ’s is using AI software to predict its labor needs and schedule its employees. Its AI sales forecasting tool was introduced in 2023, according to Restaurant Business, so this is an ongoing project. Over the last two years, BJ’s has been using AI forecasts to control its labor and food prep costs. Its restaurant-level operating margin has improved from 14.5 percent in the second quarter of 2023 to 17% in the second quarter of 2025.

But the AI sales forecasting tool isn’t just being used to cut costs. During the second-quarter earnings call, CEO Lyle Tick said that the labor model created with the AI forecasting tool also showed him that the company needed more employees during peak hours. So BJ’s may be able to gain incremental revenue by using its AI software as well.

Other cost-control initiatives include both reducing the number of items on the menu and making the items on the menu simpler to prepare. These are both ongoing projects as well. NRN provides an example of this complexity where the restaurant had 8 different ways to make a margarita. Simplifying the food prep process isn’t just reducing costs, it’s also reducing mistakes, which will improve customer satisfaction. And that’s important because another cost driver that came up in the second-quarter earnings report is linked to customer satisfaction.

Tick also mentioned that BJ’s was making progress on reducing the number of comped meals at its restaurants. While giving a customer a free meal is bad for a restaurant’s profitability, it also indicates that the customer wasn’t happy and the restaurant had to give them a free meal to make up for it, so this indicates that BJ’s is also working on the customer experience.

Risks and Positioning Around Alcohol and Other Beverages

There is a significant risk factor for BJ’s that also applies to other casual-dining restaurants. Recently, there have been many reports that younger Americans are drinking less alcohol. College students used to go to bars at night, and many still do, but now many of them go to late-night cookie bakeries like Crumbl and Insomnia instead (both privately held). Meanwhile, many restaurants have relied on alcohol sales to maintain their margins, and this restaurant has weak margins to begin with. But BJ’s could be better positioned for this trend than its peers. While this restaurant is well known for its beer, it’s also well known for its ice cream-topped cookies.

On the call, the BJ’s CEO mentioned that the restaurant’s bartenders have been mixing mocktails for customers who want non-alcoholic drinks. Lots of people still want to get together for drinks and this restaurant is still a good late-night gathering spot for groups. And the restaurant wants to focus on beverages in general rather than just alcoholic ones. But I still think the cookies give this restaurant an advantage with diners who are avoiding alcohol.

Increased Efficiency Is Appearing In Earnings

BJ’s Restaurants has beaten analysts’ earnings estimates for two quarters in a row. In the first quarter, it posted EPS of $0.58 versus a consensus of $0.40, beating the estimate by 15%. In the second quarter, it posted EPS of $0.97 versus estimates of $0.69, beating the estimate by 41%. These results suggest that analysts’ estimates for BJ’s earnings are too low. It’s also noteworthy that the second-quarter EPS beat was larger than the first-quarter one.

If BJ’s can improve its earnings by 40% because of efficiency upgrades that don’t appear to be priced in already, and the forward P/E remains the same at 20x, that would result in a price of $48 per share in a year. That’s higher than analysts’ current one-year forecast of $42 per share. BJ’s current share price is $34 per share.

Conclusion

BJ’s Restaurants appears to be trading at a discount because analysts don’t think it’ll be able to make its operations more efficient, even though both of its quarterly reports this year have shown accelerating progress toward getting its costs under control. Even without new restaurants or an increase in transaction value, it’s still likely to surpass EPS projections in upcoming quarters and this could result in a share price gain of as much as 40 percent over the next year.

data.title: Sionna Therapeutics: A Differentiated Bet In The $12 Billion Cystic Fibrosis Market
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Petri Dish Reports as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Thesis

Sionna Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:SION) for me presents a high-risk, high-reward opportunity for pharma investors, with its value today almost entirely tied to the clinical success of its NBD1 stabilizers and complementary CFTR modulators, as I will explain. The company is also pretty well-capitalized, holding over $260 million in cash, with a runway projected into 2028. Yes, the company is yet to be profitable, but with Phase 1 and Phase 2 clinical trials already showing promising results for one of its modulators. I see significant upside to this stock. The nearly $1 billion market cap and $616 million enterprise value already show me the market sees potential for Sionna in the $12 billion cystic fibrosis space. What really makes this story timely, though, is the fact that Phase 1 combination studies for SION-451 and SION-719, paired with Sionna’s proprietary correctors, are set to begin later this year, with topline data expected by mid-2026. These trials should mark the first real test of Sionna’s dual-mechanism strategy in humans. So if early signals are positive, which I expect they will be, especially on endpoints like sweat chloride or lung function, I believe this could be a major catalyst for the stock.

The next 12 to 18 months could prove pivotal for Sionna shares. What investors need is for them to demonstrate that its NBD1 stabilizers drive stronger efficacy than Vertex Pharmaceutical Incorporated's (VRTX) Trikafta, or even just work well in combination. After that then even a small slice of the CF market could translate into significant revenue down the line. For me, and for investors with a tolerance for some biotech risk, Sionna is an opportunity in the most valuable rare-disease markets. Shares have slowly risen since the April lows which saw the stock drop to sub $10. And whilst I don’t think we will see those prices again I feel there is still a lot of momentum behind this stock going into 2H25 and 2026. Year to date Sionna has actually outperformed the S&P as you can see in the Y-chart below.

Who Is Sionna Therapeutics

Sionna is a clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company. They mainly focus on developing treatments for cystic fibrosis (CF), specifically by targeting the nucleotide-binding domain 1 (NBD1) of the CFTR protein. Its a region not addressed by existing therapies. Of course, whilst the company does not yet generate revenue from product sales, it has a few leading drug candidates including SION-719 and SION-451, both of which are first-in-class NBD1 stabilizers. These compounds are designed to work somewhat as add-ons to standard of care or in dual combinations with complementary CFTR modulators, such as SION-2222 (galicaftor) and SION-109, with the goal of restoring the CFTR function more fully than current treatments. In other words,, what Sionna is trying to do is take current cystic fibrosis drugs, which already do a decent job of improving the CFTR protein function, and try to push them even further.

Certain therapies from Vertex, like Trikafta, target some of the defects caused by the F508del mutation, but they don’t completely fix the underlying protein. That’s where Sionna’s approach comes in. Their concept is that the NBD1 stabilizers, like SION-719 and SION-451, are meant to latch onto the most unstable part of the CFTR protein (the NBD1 domain) and hold it in place so the whole thing works better.

However, on their own, these stabilizers might not be enough, but when you stack them on top of existing treatments or even pair them with other CFTR modulators like SION-2222 or SION-109, you start to see this really nice, synergistic effect. The idea is that instead of just partially restoring CFTR activity, you could potentially get close to normal function, which in theory could mean better outcomes in regards to CF symptoms. They’re hoping these better outcomes could be lower sweat chloride levels and improved lung function. Outcomes such as there would of course be a much improved lifestyle for a patient. So if successful, these drugs could represent a major revenue opportunity by positioning Sionna as a competitor, or even potential complement, to Vertex’s multibillion-dollar CF franchise.

A Pathway to Profitability

Right now, Sionna doesn’t make money from selling drugs, they’re still in the clinical stage, so all of their compounds are in trials and haven’t yet been approved. So where do they get their revenue stream? Well, it mainly comes from the capital they raise. So venture funding, their $182 million Series C stock sale in 2024, and more recently, their $100 million IPO in early 2025. They also struck a licensing deal with AbbVie in mid-2024, which gave them access to those modulators I mentioned like galicaftor and navocaftor. Even though the terms of that deal weren’t fully public, we assume it involved some kind of payments or shared development rights that help fund operations. Long term, I see the money-making plan as being quite straightforward. They just need to get one or more of their NBD1 stabilizers, be it SION-719 or SION-451 through trials. After this they would have to of course pair them with their proprietary modulators, and bring to market the therapy. That being said, it's a long road but one that could rival or complement Vertex’s CF franchise. If their drugs deliver stronger efficacy than Vertex’s current standard-of-care, they could grab some market share or even be used in combination with existing therapies, both scenarios translate into significant revenue potential in my opinion.

Lets say Sionna can successfully bring one of its CFTR modulators to market. The revenue potential is huge given the size of the cystic fibrosis space. The global CF therapeutics market was worth about $10.3 billion last year. Its expected to reach about $22.7 billion by 2030, with CFTR modulators making up the bulk of sales. Vertex, which dominates the field with Trikafta and other modulators, is already pulling in over $10 billion a year from its CF franchise. So even if Sionna were to capture just a small slice of the market, it could translate into hundreds of millions in annual revenue. That would be quite significant for a company not generating any revenue.

So How Close Are Those Modulators?

Sionna’s modulators are actually steadily moving through clinical development, with SION-2222 being at the most advanced stage. It’s already completed Phase 1 and Phase 2 studies, originally conducted under AbbVie Inc. (ABBV), which showed improvements in lung function with ppFEV₁ increases of 5.2% to 7.0% and reductions in sweat chloride when paired with the potentiator ABBV-3067 or now known as SION-3067. The combination was generally well tolerated and demonstrated pharmacodynamic effects consistent with CFTR modulation. These results are pretty important because they align with existing thresholds known to be clinically meaningful in CF patients. They also serve as a validation of the modulator’s potential efficacy in Sionna’s upcoming combo trials.

SION-109, Sionna’s ICL4-directed corrector, has also cleared Phase 1 studies in healthy volunteers, where it was found to be generally safe and well tolerated. Crucially, it hit its target exposure levels, a key milestone we needed to justify advancing into combination studies.

The next step for both would of course be combination testing with Sionna’s NBD1 stabilizers (SION-451 or SION-719). These stabilizers have also recently completed Phase 1 studies, including single ascending dose, multiple ascending dose, and even food effect cohorts. Both SION-451 and SION-719 were well tolerated with no serious adverse events and reached pharmacokinetic targets that match levels shown in preclinical CFHBE models to significantly increase CFTR function when paired with modulators. In fact, preclinical data presented at ECFS 2025 (European Cystic Fibrosis Society Conference) showed that combinations of these stabilizers with either SION-2222 or SION-109 led to near-complete correction of CFTR function in cells with the F508del mutation, restoring function to wild-type levels. Its a level of correction that current standard-of-care drugs like Trikafta do not fully achieve.

Phase 1 multiple ascending dose combo trials are set to start in the second half of 2025. If things go according to plan, we should see data from these studies by mid-2026, giving investors and patients the first real look at whether this dual-mechanism strategy can meaningfully boost CFTR correction beyond today’s standard of care. I see this as a big catalyst for the stock moving forward. As we’ve seen multiple times, when non-profitable biotech stocks achieve significant Phase 1/2 results, shares tend to move in a positive direction. Put simply, SION-2222 is the most trial-ready, with SION-109 just a step behind, and both are about to be tested in the combos that could define the stock’s future movements.

Risks

Now, whilst Sionna is on a compelling growth story, I have to point out that my thesis of course carries a few risks. The biggest biggest being that the company remains in the clinical stage with no approved products, meaning its valuation is almost entirely dependent on the future success of the pipeline I outlined. Any disappointing data from the upcoming combination trials involving SION-451, SION-719, or even any of their proprietary CFTR modulators could significantly impact the stock price. Additionally, we have to remember that Sionna is competing in a space dominated by Vertex. They’re a company with a deeply entrenched commercial presence. They already have a robust distribution infrastructure set in place for their CF treatments . Even if Sionna’s therapies do show improved efficacy, gaining regulatory approval and physician adoption will take time. We saw a similar story in which Tonix Pharmaceuticals Holding Corp. (TNXP) just gained FDA approval. However, we are now seeing a pull back in their stock due to the market pricing in the long road which is drug campaigning and marketing they have ahead. There's also platform risk we have to address, the NBD1 stabilizer approach is still very novel and unproven in the clinic. Lastly, although the company currently has a strong cash runway as I mentioned, drug development is quite expensive and delays in trial progression or unexpected setbacks occur often.

Financials

In 2Q25, Sionna reported a net loss of about $18.1 million, widening from the $8.6 million in 2Q24 as operating expenses nearly doubled year over year. The big spend was on research and development, it rose to $15.4 million from $8.2 million the year prior. Whilst general and administrative expenses increased to $6.5 million from $3.1 million. Despite this higher burn, Sionna actually strengthened its balance sheet with roughly $337.3 million in cash and cash equivalents, a significant increase from the reported $168.0 million at the end of 2024. It gives them a nice cash runway expected to fund operations into 2028, of roughly 12-14 quarters from this point. Sionna’s cash burn has stepped up as that pipeline I spoke about has advanced. Over the past year, the company burned roughly $56 million, but its balance sheet still remains quite strong. Management expects that the cash runway should last well into 2028. At the least it gives Sionna a good enough runway to deliver on those clinical milestones without near-term financing pressure.

Valuation of The Stock

Sionna’s valuation right now remains tough to pin down given its in a pre-revenue stage. But we can look at a couple of metrics to see how it stacks up against the broader biotech sector. On a forward price-to-sales basis, Sionna of course has no meaningful figure to compare with that sector median of 3.80x. Similarly, its forward EV-to-sales (EV/S) multiple is non-existent versus a sector median of 3.42x. So traditional valuation ratios can’t really be applied until Sionna starts generating sales. But what it shows me is that investors are currently pricing it more on that pipeline potential and a pretty decent cash runway than on fundamentals relative to peers.

The stock currently carries a market cap of about $1.05 billion at the time of this writing, and an enterprise value of ~$720 million. On top of that they have a strong cash balance of $262.6 million. The other good news for investors is that they have quite a low debt figure of just $9.2 million. Analysts are projecting a sharp swing in earnings trajectory over the next few years with EPS growth expected at +86.8% this year. They also expect some declines in 2026, about -5.3% and -23.2% in 2027, before a recovery in 2028 +14.4%. Compared to peers like Maze Therapeutics Inc. (MAZE) or Beta Bionics, Inc. (BBNX), which also trade around the mid-cap biotech range, Sionna stands out for me because of that solid cash position, it gives them that flexibility to fund its pipeline through multiple clinical readouts. With an EV of roughly $720 million against a $12 billion plus annual CF market, investor sentiment is clearly anchored more on long-term optionality than near-term earnings.

Final Thoughts

I'll be watching the outcomes of those clinical trials closely. If its good news it will translate positively into a stock which is trading around $21. I feel this is a good entry point for investors following the CF space. In conclusion I'm quite bullish on Sionna's prospects and initiate my coverage with a buy.

Michael Kramer: It's great to be back. It feels like just yesterday we did one of these.

DS: Yeah, just a few months ago, we just talked about all of this, although not specifically realized vol and what's coming potentially, but the conversation that after you reached out was talking about let's talk about trade war 1.0 versus trade war 2.0. Are we going to see a similar vol-ish kind of market period, mimicking that trade 1.0. And then, you got a lot of correlation and things you want to mention across the board here with that. So, I would love if you could go ahead and share that presentation you put together and let's dive on into Trump 1.0 versus Trump 2.0 and all the uncertainty in the market out there.

MK: Sure. So, I have a little disclaimer, too. I'm not going to read it to you. But, if we're going to just take 10 seconds to go over it, basically, it just lightens up that this is not financial advice and you should always consult with someone. So, essentially, again, just kind of going back to where we were the last time, trade war 1.0 versus trade 2.0. And I think the key takeaway that we should really always remember is that trade war 1.0 created a lot of volatility and it created not only a lot of volatility, but it created basically no return. We saw a market peak in January of 2018 and not go anywhere until October of -- I think I said January of 2018 and not go anywhere until October of 2019.

So, there was almost a really long period of time there, almost two full years of stagnation, but just lots of volatility in the market. And so far, it's starting to look like maybe that's shaping up in a similar way. And things that really stood out to me are the volatility and the causes and the effects, both very similar. And I think this is an important concept that we're just going to want to take a moment to review.

So, right now, what we've seen is a big sharp sell-off in the equity market in 2025. And we saw a really big spike in both realized and implied volatility. And it's really important to remember, I write a lot of articles and sometimes I'm talking about volatility and sometimes what I'm referring to is not necessarily, people tend to associate volatility with the market going down. And unfortunately, that's not really the right way to think about volatility. When we talk about volatility and volatility returning, it means the market can go up or down. DS: So for somebody that's watching right now, doesn't understand what maybe the reverse repo facility is, could you summarize that real quick and why it was so much in focus over the last 4 years or 5 years?

MK: Yeah. So, I mean, the reverse repo facility, think of it more as a symbol, a symbol of excess cash. Essentially, what happened in parts of 2022 and beginning of 2023 was the Treasury started to cut back on bill issuance. And with all that cut back in bill issuance, it came at the same time that the Fed was pumping some ridiculous amount of money into the system. And essentially, there was so much excess liquidity being generated and had nowhere to go. So the way the reverse repo facility works is that you get a you're -- you're an institution. You have all this cash. You don't know what to do with it. So you give it to the Fed. In exchange, the Fed basically pays you a rate of interest. The next day comes the Fed gives you your money back. And you got paid overnight to give to lend your money to the Fed. Next day comes again. You do the same thing over and over again. And essentially, if I have a chart here, the reverse repo facility, Oh, I'm trying, which is this darker blue line goes from around 0 to around $2.5 trillion between April and January of 2022.

Now, what's important to understand is that the reverse repo facility is held on the Fed's balance sheet. And so the reverse repo facility is a liability. And so if we go back to how this works, right, we have the Fed's overall balance sheet, $9 trillion. Then you have all these little liabilities that are on the other side, currency in circulation, $2 trillion, reverse repo facility, $2.5 trillion. So as the reverse repo facility was rising from 0 until the $2.5 trillion, you saw reserve balances really, really declining in 2021 and 2022 that had a negative feedback loop into the S&P 500 as liquidity ultimately drained. And then, of course, as the reverse repo facility began to drain, that was freeing up all of the reserves that were held at the Fed.

So everyone talks about how the Fed is quantitative tightening. The Fed is quantitative tightening. And but reserve balances have really stayed unchanged because what happened was all the money that was getting locked up in the reverse repo facility and was acting as it -- was acting to drain liquidity out of the market in 2022 was basically being fed back into the market in 2023, 2024 and up until last week. And so this is why you haven't really seen reserve balances come down despite the size of the Fed balance sheet going from around $9 trillion to $6.5 trillion today, if that helps.

MK: Yeah. So First of all, I just want to emphasize, right? I'm not suggesting that anyone be 100% cash, right? What I'm suggesting is that maybe you want to have a reduced weighting in your portfolio, right? And again, none of this is investment advice, but like for me personally, the way I do it right now is that I have -- I own a lot of the Mag 7 names, right? I don't own all of them, but I own the majority of them and I bought them over the years. Right. And so, what that means to me is I've trimmed some of those positions back because they seem kind of overvalued in some ways, but I don't want to sell them all. And so, and if there's a -- and essentially what I'm trying to do is just build up a cash buffer. So, if normally I run a portfolio at 95% invested, 5% cash, Maybe I want to be at 80% invested, 20% in cash. And that's all I'm trying to mean. I just want to make sure that's clear, right? Because I think people think, oh, well, you mean you say in cash, I'm going to go 100% cash. I think that It's a horrible idea to ever be 100% in cash because generally speaking, what happens is, is that you're right, the market goes down, but then you can't get back in or the market goes up too fast and you can't get back in. I've had that, I've experienced that too. And it's a really bad experience, that's actually worse than being down. So it depends on your time frame. And I think generally, you just want to maybe reduce your exposures and put some cash aside so that if the market does pull in, you have cash to deploy.

Going to your other question on Bitcoin and gold, I do not believe in Bitcoin. In fact, I think Bitcoin is the ultimate liquidity gauge. I'm actually watching Bitcoin to tell me whether or not my theories are right or wrong. Because if I'm right, Bitcoin will be hammered just as hard as any other risk asset if we do start seeing liquidity come down. And gold is really complicated, I think because I've been watching gold really closely. I've been watching how it keeps failing at that 3,420 level. A lot of the reason why we've seen gold go up more recently is because of the dollar weakening. And I happen to think that we could be in a period where we actually start seeing the dollar strengthening a little bit against some currencies. And so I think it makes it really tough. The way I'm sort of thinking about gold and I don't own any, and I'm not really planning on buying any up at these prices, but I mean, If you want it to play it, I would just wait for the breakout above 3,425 in the spot market, because if it breaks out, it's going to run higher. And if it doesn't break out, I think it's probably got a decent way down to go.

DS: All right, Michael. So you were talking about Bitcoin and keeping an eye on that. I was pulling it up here on my other screen. Bitcoin down - is down 10% in the last seven days. I mean, is that obviously correction territory, right? Does that kind of give you a warning sign? Is that an early leading indicator in this?

MK: Yeah, I think so.

DS: 10% in seven days. That's…

MK: That’s pretty. I don't know what it's down today, but…

DS: Yeah.

MK: I would expect that it would be down.

DS: …5 right now. We'll keep an eye on that for sure.

MK: Yeah. I mean, I would expect that it probably gets worse before it gets better. It doesn't mean that it's going to go straight down. I'm not saying that because obviously liquidity, just like the market goes up and down, it changes. But you have to have a general sense of understanding where liquidity is going. And I misread liquidity in 2023 and 2024. I underestimated the pace that which the TGA, the reverse repo facility would drain and that really screwed me up. So, it's tough.

DS: Yeah. It's definitely been a few interesting years. Let me ask you about one last chess piece in this whole puzzle. And actually a few people have brought it up in questions here in the chat. And then we're going to transition over into take a little bit of Q&A here as we wrap up at the top of the hour. Liquidity drain, everybody's looking at FOMC. Everybody's looking at Jackson Hole this week specifically. Everybody's looking at September rate cuts. We're getting Fed speakers come out, whether, obviously the administration is still pressuring in multiple different ways. You had a Fed speaker come out this morning after the macroeconomic data saying don't see a need for a cut, right? Everybody's looking, is that the source of QE? Do you think QE is coming from the Fed here in the next few months? What is your take on how that last major chess piece really plays into this whole puzzle?

MK: So this is something that I've kind of hinted at with members of the service, but I really haven't publicly talked about very much because I still think it's a good nine months.

DS: No the better time than any, right? Let's hear it.

MK: Sure. Let’s spill the beans. So essentially, what's going -- what I think is going to happen is that the Fed is going to be forced into having to stop quantitative tightening, probably by year end, because I think reserve balances are going to really start getting too low. Because remember, the TGA is going to refill and it's going to leave the Fed around $3 trillion. And that's sort of getting towards the bottom buffer zone. And then, of course, they're going to continue to do QT, which is another $45 billion a month. So it's 75, you got 90, 100, and another 200 billion coming off of reserves potentially between now and year end just from QT. And so, you could be talking about reserve balances somewhere around $2.8 trillion by year end and that's really getting close.

And so, I think the Fed will probably have to finish the program at that point before year end, maybe the November meeting. But I think more importantly is that the Fed is going to then have to switch at some point beginning of next year to where they're no longer running off the balance sheet. But what they have to do, basically, from what I understand is they want to drain MBS securities. They want MBS to be zero on their books. So that means that they have to offset that. And so to offset that, it means they have to start buying treasury bills. And so, it will give the appearance that liquidity is net neutral, but that will take an -- by them starting to buy treasury bills, it will take a significant strain off of risk assets.

And if my theory is right, then what I would expect to happen is that you probably want to -- when we get this next volatility shock, if it should come, as I'm expecting, that's probably when you want to start as the market's kind of coming in and going down, that's kind of when you want to start putting cash back to work. Because when the Fed starts to buy bills, like I said, that's going to take a tremendous strain off of dealer balance sheets. And that will probably allow for the liquidity flows to start moving again. And that's probably when you get a nice rally in the market again.

DS: All right. Well, so we had Mary ask this question. As we've been talking about TGA and the reverse repo, she's wondering, is all of this also going to affect the money markets overall, where we know that there's a large, large portion of cash parked?

MK: Well, I mean, money markets are the biggest -- one of the biggest, well, they were one of the biggest participants in the reverse repo facility. And so money markets are going to be one of the biggest sources of buyers of treasury bills. And so, they're going to be involved in this for sure. I mean, money market accounts are going to be, are the ones buying a lot of the treasury bills. So I don't know that there'll be impacts. I don't know that money market accounts are going to break the buck or anything like that. I don't see that as a risk. I just think that they're going to be -- there obviously from that standpoint, they're going to be one of the ones that absorbing some of this treasury issuance.

DS: All right.

MK: I don't know if that's exactly what the question was geared towards.

DS: Yeah. I don't know, Mary, let us know if that helped answer your question. There was another question here about, is there anywhere online to access the reverse repo facility? You can actually do that on the FRED website, so just Google search for the reverse repo and check out that's updated every day. Now, Michael, there was another question about finding reserve balances. Is that also on FRED?

MK: Yeah. That comes out every Thursday at 4 30. You can -- what I do is, what I want to check it really quick in Google. I just type H.4.1 and it pulls up the first thing.

DS: That's awesome. Good to know. Let's see, we talked about rotation, potential going into bonds, going into gold, just kind of going through these questions here. ETF products in the volatility space, do you have any thoughts on ones that might be aligning with, your call for potential volatility down the road here to keep an eye on?

MK: Really don't anymore. I know we had that bad experience in 2018. And after that, I kind of just moved away from even looking at them. I think there's like the VXX or something. Is that still out there? I really don't look at them anymore.

DS: Okay. They were just asking - UVXY I think is one I saw here in the chat as well. On a fun level, what practices should we be worried about seeing managers doing right now? There's a question from Natalia. I don't know if you have any thoughts on that.

MK: Worried about doing? I mean, I can't really say what they should or shouldn't be doing. I mean, I could tell you what would make me nervous if I had my money, going towards some of these high flyers with these really crazy valuations. I don't know that I want to see those positions getting bigger. I think that, buying the dip in some of these names could be costly. Because, look, I use AI, I believe in it. And unfortunately, I think the biggest learning lesson that I ever had in my life came very early. And maybe it jaded me a little bit. But Cisco looked really cheap as it was coming down in 2001. And the problem was that it kept getting cheaper, not because the business didn't grow or because the business didn't come out to be exactly what it was expected to be.

I think Cisco pretty much at the end came out to be what it was expected to be. But the market changes it's -- the market's view changes and how much it's willing to pay for a company changes. And so, the risk here isn't that, I don't mean to pick on Palantir. I have no position. I just it's just on top of my head for some reason. There's no question that maybe one day Palantir will be everything that investors think it will be. But the question is, is whether or not the market really is going to pay 25 times sales or whatever the stupid number is in perpetuity for it, because that's the only way you keep getting it to go higher. In other words, if that valuation starts coming down, the earnings and revenue growth can continue to rise, but it may not be enough to offset the decline in the multiple. And generally speaking, as companies mature and growth rates slow, you see that market -- that multiple the market’s assigning has dropped. And that's kind of what was a really good example of what happened to Cisco, I think, too.

DS: We got a question here from, looks like Steve, your name's cut off there, but how far do you go out on the puts if you're buying? I don't know if that means how far off on the Delta or the time. I would guess the time – maybe time?

MK: I'm assuming they mean time. I mean, look, if the view is right, I mean, we're entering that period now where I think we're going to see this volatility really increase. And I think because we're in the midst of this liquidity drain, and I think we're also entering a very vulnerable period from an economic standpoint. So, I mean, I couldn't say if it's one month, two months. I mean, in essence, I think it started. I think there's a chance probably going into the beginning of October that you get some sort of relief rally just because at that point the TGA will be completely refilled and it may be that we don't really feel those effects anymore and it takes a while for some of these other effects to start hitting. So, I mean, I guess if I were to play it, I'd probably want to play something into sometime in October.

DS: That's a good, just reminded me off, we talked the other day and I had mentioned this, I should have brought it up sooner. But in regards to the TGA, we've been seeing them increase the amount of short-term issuance. Do you think they're going to continue down that path? Will they eventually push it out on the duration curve and how will that affect things going forward?

MK: Well, I mean, it seems like it because they don't want to issue, they want rates to come down first before they start going out further on the yield curve. I don't know if they're going to get that opportunity, but they may try to wait until they get a new chairman and is more willing to bring rates down. So it could very well be that, we're still sitting here next year talking about the massive amount of T-Bill issuances they're doing.

DS: All right. Well, we're about to wrap up here, but obviously I wanted to say thank you so much for the time you've given us today, Michael. I mean, going from the volatility look from 2018 to today, talking about that period of a slow grind in the markets and kind of like whipsaw until you finally get to a breakout period. But there's a lot of interesting points you brought up about liquidity. I definitely learned something here today. So I'm sure somebody else did watching the replay as well.

Everyone, if you haven't already, go check out Michael's service here on Seeking Alpha that is Reading The Markets. He is Michael Kramer from Mott Capital Management bringing us all of this data. As you mentioned, kind of like the hidden things that not everybody's talking about. And I think that's one of the really cool things that you offer within your service here on Seeking Alpha for everyone. So everybody's blowing up the chat right now saying thank you, Michael, just want you to be aware of that. And everyone have a great rest of the trading week and keep an eye on Powell because he's -- he seems to be our next big thing here at Jackson Hole. But Michael, is there anything you want to say before we jump out of here?

MK: No, the two week trial period, by the way, I'm only offering it between now and labor day after labor day, we're going to go back to paid trial. So if you want to take advantage, it's a good chance too.

DS: Awesome. Awesome. Thank you so much everyone for joining us today. Loved having you all here and we'll see you here soon in the next webinar. Take care.

MK: Bye.
